description: A 'thelai' is an ointment or salve applied to the body with the intention of soothing soreness or an irritated surface. The word describes the preparation and its purpose without promising relief or healing. 'wenu' (tonic) is drunk, while 'nepha' (medicine) covers preparations well beyond either form.
articulatory_notes: Rest the tongue tip lightly against the upper teeth for unvoiced 'th', pass air along it, and move into true-mid front unrounded 'e'. Touch the tongue tip to the alveolar ridge for voiced lateral 'l', let air pass along its sides, and open into stressed central 'a'. Keep final high-front unrounded 'i' in its own syllable across the 'a.i' hiatus.
sound_symbolism: The final vowel arrives through an open hiatus rather than a consonant closure. That gentle release can recall balm spreading under a careful hand.
usage_notes: One preparation is countable, and the same noun can refer to balm as a substance. Its source keeps the ingredients and method beside the evidence and warnings. Applying balm to another person's body also involves 'palo' (touch), so the proposed contact still needs an answer.

pillars.peace-linguistics-practices: A soothing purpose does not supply permission for touch; another person's answer still governs the application.

semantic_domains.creation: an ointment or salve prepared for soothing use
semantic_domains.physical: an external preparation intended to ease soreness or irritation
